---
title: "APIs"
subtitle: Day 19
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: ../img/horst-doorway.png
  data-background-size: 50%
  data-background-position: bottom right
  data-background-opacity: "0.8"
  data-slide-number: none
format: 
  revealjs:
    incremental: true
    code-copy: true
    code-link: true
    scrollable: true
auto-stretch: false
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)

library(countdown)
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(palmerpenguins)
library(patchwork)
library(ggthemes)
library(nycflights23)
library(here)
library(tidycensus)
slides_theme = theme_minimal(
  base_family = "Atkinson Hyperlegible",
  base_size = 16)

theme_set(slides_theme)
```


## What is an API? 

"API stands for **A**pplication **P**rogramming **I**nterface. It is a set of rules and protocols that lets different software applications communicate with each other. 

In this class, we'll use APIs to allow R to communicate with websites. These APIs focus on transmitting raw data, rather than images, fonts, etc. that humans interact with when they visit the page. 


## How do we use them? 

In this class, we're going to cover two ways to access API's: 

1. with a *wrapper* R package
2. directly with httr requests

## API wrapper packages

- To find a wrapper package search for "R package", plus the name of the website, plus "data"
    - "R package nytimes data"
    - "R package reddit data"
    - "R package weather.com data" 
    

## Census data

- Every ten years, the US Census Bureau runs a census of the US population. The goal is to survey *everybody* in the country. 
- They also run the *American Community Survey*, which tracks demographic indicators among a random sample of people every 1, 3, or 5 years
- And collect a variety of other data
    
. . . 

Importantly, the data is paid for by the public (via taxes) and is available for use by the public

## {.center}

::::: columns
::: {.column .nonincremental width="50%"}

![](https://walker-data.com/tidycensus/logo.png){width=70%}
:::

:::{.column .nonincremental width="50%"}
- R functions for accessing *some* census data

- Includes geospatial information for mapping

- Designed to work within the tidyverse ecosystem

```{r}
#| eval: false

install.packages("tidycensus")
library(tidycensus)
```

```{r}
#| echo: false

library(tidycensus)
```
:::
:::::

## Example call

::::: columns
::: {.column .nonincremental width="50%"}

```{r}
#| output: false
acs_mn_2020 <- tidycensus::get_acs(
    year = 2020,
    state = "MN",
    geography = "county",
    variables = c("B01003_001", "B19013_001"),
    output = "wide",
    geometry = TRUE
)
```

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: false 


acs_mn_2020_2 <- tidycensus::get_acs(
    year = 2024,
    state = "MN",
    geography = "county",
    variables = c("B01003_001", "B19013_001"),
    output = "wide",
    geometry = TRUE,
    show_call = TRUE
)
```

:::

::: {.column .nonincremental width="50%"}
- `geography = "tract"`: What level of detail to get summary statistics. Other options include "state", "county", "combined statistical area", etc.
- `variables = c("B01003_001", "B19013_001")`: Which survey questions should we get responses to? 
:::
:::::
## Example

```{r}
acs_mn_2020
```


## Chat with your neighbor

::: {.task .nonincremental}
- Visit <https://data.census.gov/table/> and search for the variables used in the previous example (remove everything including and after the _). What are these variables?
- Look at the help text for `get_acs()`. Look at the output on the previous slide. Why does the table show four numeric columns when we only explicitly requested two?

```{r, echo=FALSE}
countdown(4, 0)
```
:::

## Why the _001 in the variable name?

- The numeric string before the _ refers to the table, while the numeric string after the _ refers to the row in the table
- The population table only includes one row, but most tables include many
- Note: there is a function in tidycensus that helps with this (`load_variables()`), but recent changes in data management policy at the Census broke this function (they are in the process of fixing it right now though!)

## Pros of using an API: 

- Provenance: we've accessed our data directly from the source and didn't have to save it in a file format or open it in a different program
- Reproducibility: accessing data via code allows others to reproduce our work
- Customizability: we can generate as specific (or broad) of a dataset as we would like

## But, I've hidden some steps from you

- Most APIs require you to sign up for an *API key*
- This allows the website to track who is accessing their data, how many requests they are making, how often they are making them, etc. 
- Most APIs (especially if they're free) are limited in some ways
    - The Census limits the number of variables you can access in a given call to 50
    - The NYTimes API limits you to 500 requests per day and 5 requests per minute
- In addition to signing up for a key, we need to make sure R knows how to find it


## Activity - Sign up for a Census API Key

::: {.task .nonincremental}
- Go to <https://api.census.gov/data/key_signup.html> and provide your organization (Carleton College) and college email address
- Check your email. Your API key will be in the body of the email
:::

## Storing your API key

::: {.nonincremental}
1. Create a new text file in the same folder as your .qmd
2. Copy and paste your census key into the empty file
3. Save the file as `census_api_key.txt`
:::

Read in the key with the following code: 

```{r}
#| eval: false
my_key <- readLines("census_api_key.txt")
```

and tell `tidycensus` what your API key is with:

```{r}
#| eval: false
census_api_key(my_key)
```

*Do not commit and push `census_api_key.txt` to GitHub*


## More info about how census/ACS data is reported

<https://data.census.gov/>

<https://www.census.gov/acs/www/data/data-tables-and-tools/subject-tables/>



## What is `get_acs` doing underneath the hood?

```{r}
get_acs
```

## 

If we add `show_call = TRUE`, we obtain the following output: 

```{r}
#| output: false
acs_mn_2020 <- tidycensus::get_acs(
    year = 2020,
    state = "MN",
    geography = "county",
    variables = c("B01003_001", "B19013_001"),
    output = "wide",
    geometry = TRUE,
    show_call = TRUE
)
```

```
Getting data from the 2016-2020 5-year ACS
Downloading feature geometry from the Census website.  To cache shapefiles for use in future sessions, set `options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)`.
Using FIPS code '27' for state 'MN'
Census API call: https://api.census.gov/data/2020/acs/acs5?get=B01003_001E%2CB01003_001M%2CB19013_001E%2CB19013_001M%2CNAME&for=tract%3A%2A&in=state%3A27
```

## Another Example: the total number of Hmong people living in each U.S. state 

API *Query*: <https://api.census.gov/data/2019/acs/acs1?get=NAME,B02015_009E,B02015_009M‌&for=state:*>

This URL-looking-thing communicates to the Census API exactly the search terms we want in the data it returns to us

## Let's break it down:

1. Host name: https://api.census.gov/data
2. Year: https://api.census.gov/data***/2019***
3. Dataset name: https://api.census.gov/data/2019***/acs/acs1***
4. "get" https://api.census.gov/data/2019/acs/acs1***?get=***
5. Variables to get: https://api.census.gov/data/2019/acs/acs1?get=***NAME,B02015_009E,B02015_009M***
   - Get variables that are NAMEd B02015_009E (total number of Hmong people) and B02015_009E (corresponding margin of error)
6. Geographies to use:  https://api.census.gov/data/2019/acs/acs1?get=NAME,B02015_009E,B02015_009M***&for=state:* ***
   - & separates variables from geographies
   - state:* means "all states"
   
# How do we talk to the API?

## {.center}

::::: columns
::: {.column .nonincremental width="50%"}

![](https://httr2.r-lib.org/logo.png)
:::

:::{.column .nonincremental width="50%"}
- "hitter2"
- allows us to send "queries" to any API via R

```{r}
#| eval: false

install.packages("httr2")
library(httr2)
```

```{r}
#| echo: false

library(httr2)
```
:::
:::::

## Sending our census query


```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| code-line-numbers: "1"
hmong_state_request <- request("https://api.census.gov/data") %>% 
    req_url_path_append("2019") %>% 
    req_url_path_append("acs") %>% 
    req_url_path_append("acs1") %>% 
    req_url_query(get = c("NAME", "B02015_009E", "B02015_009M"), `for` = I("state:*"), key = census_api_key, .multi = "comma")
```

`request` sets the host name

## Sending our census query


```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| code-line-numbers: "2-4"
hmong_state_request <- request("https://api.census.gov/data") %>% 
    req_url_path_append("2019") %>% 
    req_url_path_append("acs") %>% 
    req_url_path_append("acs1") %>% 
    req_url_query(get = c("NAME", "B02015_009E", "B02015_009M"), `for` = I("state:*"), key = census_api_key, .multi = "comma")
```

`req_url_path_append` adds the year and dataset that we are trying to access



## Sending our census query


```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
#| code-line-numbers: "5-8"
hmong_state_request <- request("https://api.census.gov/data") %>% 
    req_url_path_append("2019") %>% 
    req_url_path_append("acs") %>% 
    req_url_path_append("acs1") %>% 
    req_url_query(get = c("NAME", "B02015_009E", "B02015_009M"), 
                  `for` = I("state:*"), 
                  key = census_api_key, 
                  .multi = "comma")
```

`req_url_query` is where we specify what to return: 

  - The `.multi` argument controls how multiple values for a given key are combined.
  - The `I()` function around `"state:*"` inhibits parsing of special characters like `:` and `*`. (It’s known as the “as-is” function.)
  - The backticks around `for` are needed because `for` is a reserved word in R (for for-loops). You’ll need backticks whenever the key name has special characters (like spaces, dashes).
  - We also tell the census what our API key is


## Results in a `<httr2_request>`

```{r}
#| echo: false

load("hmong-state-httr.Rda")
```

```{r}
hmong_state_request
```
```
<httr2_request>
GET
https://api.census.gov/data/2019/acs/acs1?get=NAME,B02015_009E,B02015_009M&for=state:*&key=4a2344XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
Body: empty
```

## Why on earth would we do this instead of using the URL

- To generalize the code with functions (this is what wrapper packages do!) 
- To handle special characters (URLs can't contain spaces)
- To iterate over multiple years, or multiple geometries, or .... 

## Actually getting the data

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
hmong_state_response <- req_perform(hmong_state_request)
hmong_state_response
```
```
<httr2_response>
GET
https://api.census.gov/data/2019/acs/acs1?get=NAME,B02015_009E,B02015_009M&for=state:*&key=4a2344XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX
Status: 200 OK
Content-Type: application/json
Body: In memory (1691 bytes)
```

## Actually getting the data in the form we can work with

```{r}
hmong_state_tbl <- hmong_state_response %>%
  resp_body_json(simplifyVector = TRUE) %>%
  janitor::row_to_names(1) %>%
  as_tibble()

hmong_state_tbl
```

## Your turn

::: {.task .nonincremental}
- Edit the `httr` code to access a new variable of your choice
- Make a `httr` request to access the 1-year ACS data from 2018, 2019, 2021, and 2022. Make sure to save your results from each call! 
- Combine all years into a single dataset
- Make a time series plot with your chosen variable on the `y-axis`, year on the `x-axis`, colored by `state.` 
    - You may want to first filter to only a few states
    - You will need to do some cleaning of the data
:::

## Neat Wrapper packages or APIs

- [SportsDataverse](https://www.sportsdataverse.org/) has a ton of R packages for accessing a variety of sports data
- [Public APIs](https://github.com/public-apis/public-apis)
- [Urban Institute's Education Data Portal](https://educationdata.urban.org/documentation/) and [R package](https://github.com/UrbanInstitute/education-data-package-r)  
- [ROpenSci](https://ropensci.org/packages/data-access/) 

## Credits/Further resources

- [Jenny Bryan's Stat545](https://stat545.com/api-wrappers.html)
- [Leslie Myint's Stat/Comp 212](https://lmyint.github.io/212_spring_2024/13-apis.html)
- [Census API User Guide](https://www.census.gov/data/developers/guidance/api-user-guide.Example_API_Queries.html#list-tab-559651575)
- [TidyCensus vignette](https://walker-data.com/tidycensus/articles/basic-usage.html)
